title: SRP-9001 EMBARK Trial Results - Primary Endpoint Miss But Positive Secondary Outcomes
text:
Data from the Phase 3 EMBARK study showed that SRP-9001 (ELEVIDYS) patients with DMD aged 4 to 7 years improved 2.6 points on the North Star Ambulatory Assessment (NSAA) total score at 52 weeks after treatment, compared to 1.9 points on placebo—a difference of 0.65 points that was not statistically significant (P = .24).

Primary Endpoint Miss: The trial failed to meet its primary efficacy endpoint, a potentially concerning result for the program. However, the failure did not prevent regulatory approval.

Positive Secondary Outcomes: Despite failing the primary endpoint, treatment resulted in robust changes on all key pre-specified functional secondary endpoints. Investigators recorded a least square mean difference of –0.64 (P = .0025) in change on time to rise (TTR) for SRP-9001-treated patients versus placebo, with more pronounced effects among 6- and 7-year-old patients.

Eligibility and Demographics: The trial enrolled 64 patients in Part 1, with 52 continuing to be followed in long-term follow-up three years after treatment. These patients demonstrated sustained functional improvements over time.

FDA Regulatory Update: The FDA accepted Sarepta's efficacy supplement for SRP-9001, granting priority review with a decision expected by June 21, 2024, with the goal of expanding the labeled indication to treat patients with DMD who have a confirmed mutation in the DMD gene.

Safety Profile: There were no new observed safety signals in the trial. The most common treatment-related adverse events were gastrointestinal events including vomiting, nausea, and decreased appetite, as well as pyrexia. Overall safety profile was consistent with SRP-9001 to date.
